description: This figure plots the polarization resistance (Rₚ, unit: Ω·cm²) of surface oxide films on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) as a function of immersion time (0 to 168 h) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, with Rₚ values derived from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) fittings. Four tested samples include S0 (no post-rolling solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃, fine-grained with average grain size 8.7 μm), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃, medium-grained), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃, coarse-grained with average grain size 32 μm). The fine-grained S950 sample exhibits the highest Rₚ across the entire immersion period, reaching ~1.15×10⁵ Ω·cm² at 168 h, which is significantly higher than the ~5×10⁴ Ω·cm² of coarse-grained S1150. Rₚ of S950 rises rapidly in the initial 2-8 h of immersion and stabilizes after approximately 24 h, indicating fast formation of a dense, stable protective oxide film. Coarser-grained samples show lower initial Rₚ and slower Rₚ growth, corresponding to delayed oxide film formation and inferior protective performance. This figure supports the core conclusion that grain refinement of LDSs improves corrosion resistance by promoting rapid formation of protective Al-rich oxide films.
